description:
You are an infrastructure engineer automating a small part of a bare-metal provisioning workflow.  
    Two comma-separated value (CSV) files have already been staged for you under  
    /home/user/provisioning/input/  :

    1. /home/user/provisioning/input/servers.csv  
       Contents **exactly** (including the header row, no extra blank lines):
       host,ip,cpu,ram
       web01,10.10.1.11,4,16
       db01,10.10.1.12,8,32
       cache01,10.10.1.13,2,8

    2. /home/user/provisioning/input/network.csv  
       Contents **exactly**:
       ip,vlan,subnet
       10.10.1.11,frontend,10.10.1.0/24
       10.10.1.12,backend,10.10.1.0/24
       10.10.1.13,cache,10.10.1.0/24

    Both files are already sorted on the “ip” column, so you do **not** need to sort them.

    Task
    ----
    1. Using a single pipeline made only of standard text-processing tools available on a minimal Linux install (e.g. `cut`, `paste`, **no** external scripting language), build a third CSV file named  
       /home/user/provisioning/output/provisioning_map.csv

    2. The resulting file MUST contain exactly **four** lines (three data rows plus a header) with the following columns in this precise order:

       host,ip,vlan
       web01,10.10.1.11,frontend
       db01,10.10.1.12,backend
       cache01,10.10.1.13,cache

       • Column delimiter: a single comma (`,`).  
       • No leading or trailing spaces anywhere.  
       • Final newline after the last line is required.  
       • No extra blank lines.

    3. You may assume the parent directory /home/user/provisioning/output/ already exists and is writable.  
       If the file provisioning_map.csv already exists, overwrite it.

    4. Do **not** manually edit or open an interactive editor; rely solely on a concise command (or pipeline) that combines column selection and pasting.

    Verification
    ------------
    The automated test will:
    • Confirm that /home/user/provisioning/output/provisioning_map.csv exists.  
    • Perform a byte-for-byte comparison against the exact four-line output shown above.  

    Write only the commands you would type in the terminal to accomplish the task.
